title: Signals, Traps, and Guaranteed Cleanup
description:
## Make the temp directory always get cleaned up

A long-running job creates a temporary working directory and must remove it no matter how the script ends — normal exit, an error under `set -e`, or the operator pressing Ctrl-C. The author also wants a counter, incremented inside a pipeline, to survive after the pipeline finishes:

```bash
tmp=$(mktemp -d)
count=0
somecmd | while read -r line; do count=$((count+1)); done
echo "$count"
```

Which approach is correct?

options.text: Use `trap 'rm -rf "$tmp"' EXIT` — the EXIT trap runs on normal exit, on `exit`, on errexit, and (because the default INT/TERM handling terminates the shell, which fires EXIT) covers Ctrl-C too; meanwhile `count` prints `0` because the `while` runs in a subshell of the pipeline, so its variable updates are lost in the parent.
Use `trap 'rm -rf "$tmp"' KILL` so that even `kill -9` is intercepted; `count` will print the correct total because Bash runs every pipeline stage in the current shell.

Only `trap 'rm -rf "$tmp"' INT TERM` is needed; a normal exit does not require a trap because Bash auto-removes anything created by `mktemp`, and `count` is correct because `while` does not start a subshell.
Register `trap 'rm -rf "$tmp"' EXIT INT TERM KILL` to be safe; the `count` value is reliable as long as `pipefail` is set.

explanation: An `EXIT` trap is the canonical "always run" cleanup: it fires on normal completion, on explicit `exit`, and when `set -e` aborts the script. A Ctrl-C sends SIGINT; the default action terminates the shell, and shell termination runs the EXIT trap — so a single `trap ... EXIT` already covers the interactive-interrupt case (you may additionally trap INT/TERM if you want custom behaviour before exiting). SIGKILL (signal 9) and SIGSTOP **cannot be caught, blocked, or ignored** by any process, so trapping `KILL` is meaningless — this eliminates (b) and (d). mktemp does **not** auto-clean its output, and Bash does not auto-remove temp dirs, so (c) is wrong on cleanup. The second issue is the classic subshell pitfall: in a pipeline, each stage (including the `while`) runs in its own child process, so `count` is incremented in a subshell and the parent still sees `0` — (a) describes this correctly. (Bash's `lastpipe` option, with job control off, would run the last stage in the current shell, but it is not enabled by default.) Options (b)/(c) wrongly claim the while body runs in the current shell.

hints.title: Which signals are uncatchable
hints.body: SIGKILL (9) and SIGSTOP cannot be trapped, blocked, or ignored. Any cleanup that 'handles KILL' is fiction.

hints.title: EXIT covers a lot
hints.body: An EXIT trap runs for normal exit, explicit exit, and errexit aborts. Because the default SIGINT action terminates the shell, EXIT also fires on Ctrl-C.

hints.title: Pipelines spawn subshells
hints.body: Each stage of a pipeline runs in a child process by default, so variables modified in a piped while-read loop don't propagate to the parent unless you use lastpipe or process substitution.
